full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is easy every Advent to romanticize prophets as the people whose warnings we heed,<|8.32|> <|9.08|> as the north stars of church and society.<|13.00|> <|14.66|> In practice, however, the prophets' warnings often go unheeded<|21.16|> <|21.16|> and are treated as unserious, unattainable, or laughable.<|27.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Every Advent, preachers focus on the prophets' lyrical visions and poetic demonstrations without asking what unheeded prophecy says about God, the one sending the prophets in the first place.<|19.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I say this from experience.<|3.40|> <|3.94|> I have preached almost every Advent to the past eight years,<|9.32|> <|9.32|> and in reviewing those sermon texts, I have done exactly that.<|14.34|> <|15.62|> So what does it say about God that God warns us about injustice and sin,<|22.58|> <|22.88|> knowing full well that we may ignore God altogether?<|26.98|> <|26.98|> Amen.<|27.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What does it say about God that many, if not most, prophets end up being either killed, silenced, or persecuted?<|10.04|> <|12.16|> If Apple made a product as fault-prone as the prophets, if I'm to compare them to products, the company would fold.<|22.74|> <|23.78|> Fold.<|24.24|> <|24.24|> Fold.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Is God a failure?<|2.00|> <|3.36|> Should God go back to the drawing board, bring in a few consultants, and reassess this strategy?<|10.22|> <|13.62|> Notice where John the Baptist carries out his prophetic work.<|18.88|> <|18.88|> Notice.<|19.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When you get home, do like I did last week, turn to the map section of your Bible, and you will see the region around the Jordan River is precisely as the narrator describes it.<|16.06|> <|16.96|> It's wilderness.<|17.96|> <|17.96|> It's wilderness.<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> John, like many of the prophets before him, listened to God and announced God's message at the edge, not the center of society.<|11.32|> <|12.76|> Instead of using constant contact, MailChimp, or an instant messaging app...<|19.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God speaks to John the Baptist in the middle of nowhere about a vision that will bring God's mercy and forgiveness everywhere.<|10.00|> <|11.68|> Throughout scripture, the wilderness is a literary motif that nods to the intimacy and wildness of life with God.<|21.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Israelites spend 40 years in the wilderness, acting out decades of tug of war between dead idols and the living God.<|9.18|> <|10.38|> After his baptism, Jesus spends 40 days in the wilderness, confronting the devil's alluring shortcuts to fame, power, and wealth.<|20.80|> <|20.80|> Amen.<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And John the Baptist spends a period in the wilderness inviting first century Israel to return to their first love.<|8.66|> <|10.58|> But, and you know the rest of the story, when biblical characters awaken to their belovedness in God,<|18.62|> <|19.02|> people, places, and things crowd that sense of belovedness out and they, we,<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> forget who and whose we are.<|3.08|> <|4.96|> Which brings us again to our original question.<|9.02|> <|11.40|> Is God a failure if God's overtures of love are ignored, thwarted, or rejected outright?<|21.94|> <|21.94|> Is God a failure if God's overtures of love are ignored?<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Perhaps an answer to this question is found in what we all know as the vulnerability of love.<|8.96|> <|10.38|> Love that finds its genesis in one person or community when shared does not have to be reciprocated.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That is the risk inherent to any and every relationship.<|5.38|> <|6.88|> To love is to open oneself not only to the possibility of having one's love returned,<|13.84|> <|13.84|> but also to the possibility of being hurt and disappointed.<|19.52|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> created.<|1.28|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When God loves, it is no different.<|5.14|> <|6.14|> When God loves, God is saying, I might end up hurt, but my love for you will not be extinguished.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God's persistence in sending prophets throughout history is God's way of saying,<|7.42|> <|7.90|> I will not give up on you.<|10.52|> <|11.42|> The love that I have for you is impenetrable, indissoluble, and unbreakable.<|17.30|> <|17.68|> Ignore me if you will, but love will outlast all your efforts to distract yourself from the voice of love at the center of your being.<|27.76|> <|28.58|> Amen.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But what of the prophets?<|3.62|> <|5.14|> What comes of the John the Baptist, the Elijah's, the Anna's, the Deborah's of the world?<|11.74|> <|11.74|> Is a prophet's life wasted if their message remains largely unheeded?<|18.24|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.66|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> While this is counterintuitive in our culture of hyper-individualism, a prophet is actually very rarely an individual.<|10.98|> <|12.74|> Usually, a prophet's singular name is a stand-in for a whole community of people.<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For example, while we often perk up at the sound of the Reverend Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s voice,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> it was common working class people who made the Southern Freedom Movement possible.<|17.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes, Dr. King was often the one covered on television and in newspapers,<|6.20|> <|6.20|> but it was common working people who acted.<|10.92|> <|13.04|> Prophets are like red ants.<|15.12|> <|16.12|> If you see one, a thousand are close by.<|19.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A prophet is only a prophet through the prophetic orientation of other people.<|6.62|> <|9.02|> Prophecy takes place in community.<|11.66|> <|13.50|> In 1 Corinthians, St. Paul goes so far as to say,<|17.86|> <|17.94|> and I'm giving you the BGV, the Broderick Greer version,<|21.10|> <|22.06|> prophesy in community.<|24.10|> <|24.10|> Prophesy.<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When you prophesy, do it in the company of the baptized so their eyes and ears can enhance yours.<|8.46|> <|9.52|> The vision of God does not belong to a single person.<|13.44|> <|15.64|> So when a prophet and their message is ignored, rejected, or thwarted,<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The prophet has a community of belonging because God's love, however vulnerable, cannot be wasted or hoarded.<|10.16|> <|12.72|> God's economy cannot be measured according to shallow, short-sighted metrics.<|21.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I can see in my sanctified imagination,<|4.54|> <|5.18|> St. John the Baptist, the cousin of our Lord Jesus,<|8.30|> <|8.96|> resting on a pillow after a long day of baptizing<|12.94|> <|12.94|> and prophesying in the wilderness.<|15.04|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Yes.<|1.66|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Then an abrasive, nagging voice inside him asks,<|6.10|> <|6.10|> if your message is from God, why aren't you more successful?<|11.28|> <|14.02|> And I could hear a quieter, more sturdy voice say,<|20.00|> <|21.08|> that's not how love works.<|23.92|> <|25.64|> Rest.<|26.04|> <|26.04|> Rest.<|27.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.40|><|endoftext|>
